metadata.note: Top 10 most common proof strategies. A single proof may use multiple strategies. Counts reflect the number of proofs in which each strategy appears as a key technique.

proof_strategies.name: Direct Algebraic Computation / Chain of Equalities
proof_strategies.description: The proof proceeds by directly computing and simplifying algebraic expressions step by step, often presented as a chain of equalities. This includes verifying identities, expanding products, and applying known algebraic rules to reach the desired conclusion.

examples.summary: Proves associativity of addition mod n by a direct chain of equalities: (a̅+b̅)+c̅ = ... = a̅+(b̅+c̅), using associativity of integer addition.
Proves -u is a unit by directly computing (-v)(-u)=vu=1 and (-u)(-v)=uv=1.
Proves G is abelian by direct computation: ab=(ab)^{-1}=b^{-1}a^{-1}=ba.

proof_strategies.name: Proof by Contradiction
proof_strategies.description: Assume the negation of the desired conclusion (or assume a hypothesis that should fail), then derive a logical contradiction—such as violating minimality, exceeding group order via element counts, or producing an impossible arithmetic equation.

examples.summary: Assumes x^a = x^b for distinct 0 ≤ a < b ≤ n-1, derives x^{b-a}=1 with 1 ≤ b-a < n, contradicting minimality of the order n.
Assumes x²−√2 is reducible over Z[√2], substitutes a root a+b√2, derives 2ab=1 for integers a,b—a contradiction.
Euclid-style: assumes finitely many primes ≡ 3 (mod 4), constructs a=4p₂⋯pₙ+3 not divisible by any of them, uses a lemma to get a contradiction.

proof_strategies.name: Sylow Theorem Counting Arguments
proof_strategies.description: Uses the Sylow theorems to constrain the number n_p of Sylow p-subgroups: n_p ≡ 1 (mod p) and n_p divides [G:P]. When the constraints force n_p = 1, the unique Sylow p-subgroup is normal. Often combined with element counting when multiple primes are involved.

examples.summary: |G|=351=3².13. n₁₃ = 1+13k must divide 9, forcing k=0, so the unique 13-Sylow subgroup is normal.
|G|=462=11·42. n₁₁ ≡ 1 (mod 11) and n₁₁ | 42, forcing n₁₁=1, so the Sylow 11-subgroup is normal.
|G|=105=3·5·7. Systematically constrains n₇, n₅, n₃ using Sylow counting and element counting to show at least one Sylow subgroup is normal.

proof_strategies.name: Conjugation and Normality Arguments
proof_strategies.description: Proves a subgroup is normal by directly showing gHg⁻¹ ⊆ H for all g ∈ G, or uses conjugation to establish commutativity or centrality of elements. The key step is showing that conjugating any element of the subgroup keeps it within the subgroup.

examples.summary: Shows H∩K is normal: for x ∈ H∩K, gxg⁻¹ ∈ H (since H ◁ G) and gxg⁻¹ ∈ K (since K ◁ G), so gxg⁻¹ ∈ H∩K.
Shows intersection of normal subgroups is normal: for n ∈ ∩N_α, gng⁻¹ ∈ N_α for each α, hence gng⁻¹ ∈ ∩N_α.
Shows element of order 2 is central: conjugate z=y⁻¹xy has z²=e, so z=x (unique element of order 2), giving xy=yx.

proof_strategies.name: Order, Index, and Divisibility Arguments (Lagrange's Theorem)
proof_strategies.description: Uses the fact that the order of a subgroup divides the order of the group (Lagrange), or that the index factors as [G:H] = [G:K][K:H], or that the order of an element divides the group order, to constrain possibilities and draw conclusions.

examples.summary: A subgroup of index p (the smallest prime dividing |G|) is normal—a direct index argument.
Uses |a| divides |(Z/pZ)×| = p-1 (Lagrange) to prove Fermat's little theorem: a^p ≡ a (mod p).
Uses [G:P]=[G:H][H:P] so if p∤[G:P] then p∤[H:P], showing P is also a Sylow p-subgroup of H.

proof_strategies.name: Quotient Groups and Isomorphism Theorems
proof_strategies.description: Uses the First, Second, or Third Isomorphism Theorem, or constructs and analyzes quotient structures (G/H, R/I) to transfer information between groups/rings. Includes passing to quotients to simplify analysis and using canonical projections.

examples.summary: Uses the Second Isomorphism Theorem: H_{i+1}/H_i ≅ H_{i+1}G_i/G_i ≤ G_{i+1}/G_i, so each factor is abelian.
Passes to G/P, analyzes Sylow subgroups of the quotient, then pulls back via the canonical map π to get normal subgroups of G.
Uses the First Isomorphism Theorem: G/K ↪ S_p, so |G/K| divides p!, constraining the kernel K.

proof_strategies.name: Element Counting / Pigeonhole Principle
proof_strategies.description: Counts elements of specific orders or in specific substructures to derive constraints, often leading to a contradiction (too many elements) or establishing the size of a quotient/subgroup. Includes pairing arguments and coset counting.

examples.summary: Counts elements of order 5, 7, and 3 in G of order 105; 90 of order 7 + 96 of order 5 = 186 > 105, a contradiction.
Pairs each element g with g⁻¹; since |G| is even and e is unpaired, there must be at least one other element a with a=a⁻¹, i.e., a²=e.
Counts vectors in v+Fu to bound |F| ≤ n-1 when V is a union of n proper subspaces.

proof_strategies.name: Double Inclusion / Biconditional Proof (Prove Both Directions)
proof_strategies.description: Proves set equality A = B by showing A ⊆ B and B ⊆ A separately, or proves an 'if and only if' statement by proving both implications (⟹ and ⟸) independently.

examples.summary: Proves (2x) = (2)∩(x) by showing (2x) ⊆ (2)∩(x) and (2)∩(x) ⊆ (2x) with separate containment arguments.
Proves A×B abelian ⟺ A and B abelian, with separate (⟹) and (⟸) arguments using component-wise computation.
Proves x²=1 ⟺ |x| divides 2, with (⟹) and (⟸) handled separately.

proof_strategies.name: Counterexample / Explicit Construction
proof_strategies.description: Proves existence (or disproves universality) by constructing a specific, concrete example that satisfies (or violates) the required conditions. Includes brute-force verification over finite sets.

examples.summary: Shows ⋆ on Z is not commutative by computing 1⋆(−1) = 2 ≠ −2 = (−1)⋆1.
Constructs Klein four group G={e,a,b,ab} and H={e,a}; defines automorphism σ swapping a↔b to show H is normal but not characteristic.
Checks all elements of F₃₁ by brute force to verify x³−9 has no roots, proving irreducibility.

proof_strategies.name: Case Analysis
proof_strategies.description: Breaks the proof into exhaustive cases based on different conditions or values of a parameter, and handles each case separately.

examples.summary: Considers three cases for z=a+bi ∈ I: (1) b=0, (2) a=0, (3) both a,b ≠ 0, handling each separately.
Cases |x|=1 and |x|=2 are handled separately in the (⟸) direction.
Handles cases: |x| infinite, |x| and |g⁻¹xg| both finite, then shows m=n by mutual divisibility.
